go faster"},{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/arrays","name":"arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, s]"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-11-10T21:07:32Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T22:32:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"While playing around with numpy arrays from julia ([discource thread](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/improving-parallel-loop-performance-by-using-numpy-for-allocations/122420?u=artemsolod)) I have noticed that code like `copy_np` outperforms `copy_jl` ([full benchmark here](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/improving-parallel-loop-performance-by-using-numpy-for-allocations/122420/3)

```julia
using PythonCall
np = pyimport("numpy")

function copy_jl(arr)
    sum(copy(arr))
end

function copy_np(arr)
    pymem = np.empty(length(arr))
    pyarr = PyArray(pymem)
    pyarr .= arr
    ans = sum(pyarr)
    return ans
end
```

Further exploration revealed that disabling [hugepages](https://docs.kernel.org/admin-guide/mm/transhuge.html) in `numpy` removes this gap `ENV["NUMPY_MADVISE_HUGEPAGE"] = "0"`. Apparently `numpy` uses hugepages by default for large arrays ([docs](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/global_state.html#madvise-hugepage-on-linux) and [source](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/7c0e2e4224c6feb04a2ac4aa851f49a2c2f6189f/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/alloc.c#L111)]). If I understand correctly `/sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled` is normally not set to `always` by default but rather to `madvise` which requires the underlying code to explicitly request this functionality - I have checked on Ubuntu 24.10 and 22.04.

Can we have this in julia as well? Given people are routinely working with large arrays (and adoption in numpy) this will likely be useful at least as an option. I did some experiments and they indicate that manually constructing arrays from memory that was `madvise`'d for hugepage shows good improvement:

timings
```
julia +release -t 1 copyadd_hugepage.jl 
... precomps
  0.043465 seconds (3 allocations: 76.294 MiB) # pure julia
  0.043485 seconds (3 allocations: 76.294 MiB) # malloc
  0.021253 seconds (3 allocations: 76.294 MiB) # malloc + madivse(MADV_HUGEPAGE)
```
source
```julia
import Mmap: MADV_HUGEPAGE

function malloc_hugepage_vector_allocator(nels; hugepage=true)
    nbytes = (nels * 8)
    mlcd = @ccall pvalloc(nbytes::Csize_t)::Ptr{Cvoid} # depreceated pvalloc
    if hugepage
            success = @ccall madvise(mlcd :: Ptr{Cvoid}, nbytes :: Csize_t, MADV_HUGEPAGE :: Cint)  :: Cint
            @assert iszero(success)
    end
    arr_cons = @ccall jl_ptr_to_array_1d(
        Vector{Float64}::Any,
        mlcd::Ptr{Cvoid},
        nels::Csize_t,
        1::Cint # 1 triggers gc
    ) :: Vector{Float64}
    return arr_cons
end

function copyadd_jl(arr)
    arr1 = Vector{Float64}(undef, length(arr))
    arr1 .= arr
    return sum(arr)
end

function copyadd_hugepage(arr; hugepage=true)
    arr1 = malloc_hugepage_vector_allocator(length(arr); hugepage)
    arr1 .= arr
    return sum(arr)
end

GC.enable(false)
arr_test = ones(10_000_000)

@time copyadd_jl(arr_test)
@time copyadd_hugepage(arr_test; hugepage=false)
@time copyadd_hugepage(arr_test; hugepage=true)

@time copyadd_jl(arr_test)
@time copyadd_hugepage(arr_test; hugepage=false)
@time copyadd_hugepage(arr_test; hugepage=true)
```

```julia

and on nightly it constructs an invalid name `B = Union{B_Immut{X}, B_Mut{X}}`. See https://github.com/Tortar/HybridStructs.jl/actions/runs/11061551686/job/30734388810.

end
```

Also, this instead works

```julia

julia> @hybrid struct B end
```

syntax","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-09-26T23:19:14Z","updated_at":"2024-09-27T18:12:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm updating one package and something strange is happening, I think it should be a bug in Julia. this is the PR in question https://github.com/Tortar/HybridStructs.jl/pull/3, the test code is valid and can be executed, but it actually throws in Julia 1.10 and Julia 1.11

```julia
ERROR: LoadError: syntax: unsupported `const` declaration on local variable around /home/runner/work/HybridStructs.jl/HybridStructs.jl/src/HybridStructs.jl:86
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/work/HybridStructs.jl/HybridStructs.jl/test/runtests.jl:11
 [2] include(fname::String)
   @ Base.MainInclude ./client.jl:489
 [3] top-level scope
   @ none:6
in expression starting at /home/runner/work/HybridStructs.jl/HybridStructs.jl/test/runtests.jl:11
Package HybridStructs errored during testing
Error: Process completed with exit code 1.
```

and on nightly it constructs an invalid name `B = Union{B_Immut{X}, B_Mut{X}}`. See https://github.com/Tortar/HybridStructs.jl/actions/runs/11061551686/job/30734388810.

For a reproducer you can dev that branch and execute

```julia
using HybridStructs
@hybrid struct B{X} end # this errors
```

while you can see that this is valid code

```julia
julia> @macroexpand @hybrid struct B{X} end
quote
    #= /home/bob/.julia/dev/HybridStructs/src/HybridStructs.jl:83 =#
    mutable struct B_Mut{X} <: Any
        #= /home/bob/.julia/dev/HybridStructs/src/HybridStructs.jl:49 =#
        #= REPL[6]:1 =#
    end
    #= /home/bob/.julia/dev/HybridStructs/src/HybridStructs.jl:84 =#
    struct B_Immut{X} <: Any
        #= /home/bob/.julia/dev/HybridStructs/src/HybridStructs.jl:53 =#
        #= REPL[6]:1 =#
    end
    #= /home/bob/.julia/dev/HybridStructs/src/HybridStructs.jl:85 =#
    const B = Union{B_Immut{X}, B_Mut{X}} where X
    #= /home/bob/.julia/dev/HybridStructs/src/HybridStructs.jl:86 =#
    nothing
end
```

Also, this instead works

```julia
julia> using HybridStructs
julia> @hybrid struct B end
```

```julia
julia> macro repro()
           e = :(const B = X
                     where X)
           return esc(quote $e end)
       end
@repro (macro with 1 method)

julia> @repro
ERROR: syntax: unsupported `const` declaration on local variable around REPL[1]:4
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[2]:1

julia> macro repro()
           e = :(const B = X where X)
           return esc(quote $e end)
       end
@repro (macro with 1 method)

julia> @repro
Any
```

Seems like the newline character breaks the expression when ina macro somehow

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-09-28T16:49:14Z","updated_at":"2024-09-29T00:58:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55920","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55920.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"We have `!f === (!)∘f` to negate any boolean function. Was there ever discussion of making `-f === (-)∘f` too? Both are small conveniences to avoid defining `x -> op(x)`. This one would allow for example:

```julia
julia> A = reshape(1:16,4,4);

julia> eigvals(A; sortby=-real)  # does not accept rev=true
4-element Vector{ComplexF64}:
     36.209372712298546 + 0.0im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 + 1.9341169324764503e-16im
 -4.146487327145629e-17 - 1.9341169324764503e-16im
     -2.209372712298546 + 0.0im

julia> sum(-log, A; dims=1)  # -sum allocates twice
1×4 Matrix{Float64}:
 -3.17805  -7.42655  -9.38261  -10.6846
```

This will not change the fact that `-f.(xs)` means `-(f.(xs))`, when usually fused `@. -f(xs)` would be better. (Unless you write `(-f).(xs)` by hand.)

Needs tests obviously, but RFC?

#54157 

This PR adds a function which can be useful when one wants to infer the eltype of a collection with only a loose `eltype` but which could be made tighter by using type inference, for example:

```julia
julia> struct A end

julia> struct B end

julia> itr = (x < 5 ? A() : B() for x in 1:10);

julia> eltype(itr)
Any

julia> infer_eltype(itr)
Union{A, B}
```

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-08-27T11:19:36Z","updated_at":"2024-08-28T19:22:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55599","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55599","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55599.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55599.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"As of now, there's no way to access default field values passed to @kwdef, aside from parsing the constructor signature (https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-to-get-default-values-of-a-functions-kwargs/66158/2).

This PR adds `Base.kwdef_defaults(::Type) = (default fieldvals)` for all structs defined with `@kwdef`. This function can either be made public immediately, or remain internal for some time.

Fixes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50876. Useful in various cases, for example in pretty-printing: knowing the defaults makes it possible for user show() methods to omit fields with default values.

That's what `@kwdef` now expands to:
```julia
julia> @macroexpand @kwdef struct S
               a = 1
               b::Int = 2
               c
       end
quote
    #= REPL[22]:51 =#
    begin
        $(Expr(:meta, :doc))
        struct S
            #= REPL[25]:2 =#
            a
            #= REPL[25]:3 =#
            b::Int
            #= REPL[25]:4 =#
            c
        end
    end
    #= REPL[22]:52 =#
    function S(; a = 1, b = 2, c)
        #= REPL[25]:1 =#
        S(a, b, c)
    end
    #= REPL[22]:53 =#
    Base.kwdef_defaults(::Base.Type{S}) = begin
            #= REPL[22]:53 =#
            (; a = 1, b = 2)
        end
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55599/reactions","total_count":7,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":3,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55599/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2316090179","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55599#issuecomment-2316090179","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55599","id":2316090179,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6KDLtD","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-28T19:22:37Z","updated_at":"2024-08-28T19:22:37Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"For some reflection tasks I would actually like to access also the function in cases like @jw3126 pointed out, couldn't we add another function for this? something like

faster"},{"id":421532175,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw0MjE1MzIxNzU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/compiler:inference","name":"compiler:inference","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":"Type inference"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2021-05-26T00:35:36Z","updated_at":"2024-07-02T22:27:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This a simplified example of [this thread](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/union-splitting-vs-c/61772/17) on discourse, but which appears to have revealed an inefficient behaviour of one of the common iterators when dealing with arrays of mixed Union types. Here is the simplified example:

```
using BenchmarkTools, Test

struct A x::Float64 end
struct B x::Float64 end
struct C x::Float64 end
struct D x::Float64 end
struct E x::Float64 end

function sum1(v)
  s = 0.
  for x in v
    s += x.x
  end
  s
end

function sum2(v)
  s = 0.
  for (i,x) in pairs(v)
    s += x.x
  end
  s
end

t = [A,B,C,D,E]
v = Union{A,B,C,D,E}[rand(t)(rand()) for i in 1:1000]

@test sum1(v) ≈ sum2(v)

@btime sum1($v)

@btime sum2($v)
```

result (Julia 1.6.1):

```
  241.063 μs (7980 allocations: 140.31 KiB)
  992.583 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
501.12183238665824
```

Thus, iterating with `for x in v` is much worse than using `for (i,x) in pairs(v)`, which does not appear to make any sense in terms of functionality.

inference"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2021-05-26T00:35:36Z","updated_at":"2024-07-02T23:35:37Z","closed_at":"2024-07-02T22:41:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This a simplified example of [this thread](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/union-splitting-vs-c/61772/17) on discourse, but which appears to have revealed an inefficient behaviour of one of the common iterators when dealing with arrays of mixed Union types. Here is the simplified example:

```
using BenchmarkTools, Test

struct A x::Float64 end
struct B x::Float64 end
struct C x::Float64 end
struct D x::Float64 end
struct E x::Float64 end

function sum1(v)
  s = 0.
  for x in v
    s += x.x
  end
  s
end

function sum2(v)
  s = 0.
  for (i,x) in pairs(v)
    s += x.x
  end
  s
end

t = [A,B,C,D,E]
v = Union{A,B,C,D,E}[rand(t)(rand()) for i in 1:1000]

@test sum1(v) ≈ sum2(v)

@btime sum1($v)

@btime sum2($v)
```

result (Julia 1.6.1):

```
  241.063 μs (7980 allocations: 140.31 KiB)
  992.583 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
501.12183238665824
```

Thus, iterating with `for x in v` is much worse than using `for (i,x) in pairs(v)`, which does not appear to make any sense in terms of functionality.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55096","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55096/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55096/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55096/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55096","id":2401246428,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6PIBzc","number":55096,"title":"Possible improvement in memory storage of `Union` of immutable structs","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-10T16:53:26Z","updated_at":"2024-07-10T16:53:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I noticed that an array of a Union of types when types are wrapped is able to compactify the storage more than when the types are not wrapped, as an example which I tested both on 1.11 and nightly:

```julia
using Random, BenchmarkTools

@kwdef struct A
    common_field::Int = 1
    a::Bool = true
    b::Int = 1
end
@kwdef struct B
    common_field::Int = 2
    c::Int = 1
    d::Float64 = rand(rng, Float64)
    e::Complex{Float64} = 1. + 1.0im
end
@kwdef struct C
    common_field::Int = 1
    f::Float64 = 1.0
    g::Bool = true
    h::Float64 = rand(rng, Float64)
    i::Complex{Float64} = 1. + 1.0im
end
@kwdef struct D
    common_field::Int = 1
    l::String = "a"
end

struct AT
    v::Union{A,B,C,D}
end

rng = MersenneTwister(42);
xs_wrap = [rand(rng, (AT(A()), AT(B()), AT(C()), AT(D()))) for _ in 1:10000];
rng = MersenneTwister(42);
xs_no_wrap = [rand(rng, (A(), B(), C(), D())) for _ in 1:10000];

println("Array size with wrapping: $(Base.summarysize(xs_wrap)) bytes\n")
println("Array size without wrapping: $(Base.summarysize(xs_no_wrap)) bytes\n")
```

returns

```julia
Array size with wrapping: 340161 bytes
Array size without wrapping: 399753 bytes
```

if you remove the randomness of the element values completely yo will get

```julia
Array size with wrapping: 120753 bytes
Array size without wrapping: 399961 bytes
```

is there a way to have the same kind of optimization without the wrapping?

performance of `sum(Base.Broadcast.Broadcasted(*, (x, z)))` when `z` has eltype `Union{Float64, Missing}` regressed dramatically between Julia 1.3 and 1.4. It improved again in 1.5 but it's still much slower on master than on 1.3.

On Julia 1.3.1:
```julia
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> using LinearAlgebra

julia> x = rand(10_000);

julia> z = Vector{Union{Float64, Missing}}(x);

julia> f(A, w) = sum(Base.Broadcast.Broadcasted(*, (A, w)))
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(x, z);
  31.150 μs (11 allocations: 240 bytes)
```

On 1.4.2:
```julia
julia> @btime f(x, z);
  2.070 ms (40004 allocations: 1.07 MiB)
```

On master:
```julia
julia> @btime f(x, z);
  941.751 μs (40002 allocations: 1.07 MiB)
```

Cc: @tkf. call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-06-23T22:58:29Z","updated_at":"2024-07-18T12:31:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54909","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54909","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54909.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54909.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Addresses #54157 

This PR adds a function which can be useful when one wants to infer the eltype of a collection with only a loose `eltype` but which could be made tighter by using type inference, for example:

```julia
julia> struct A end

julia> struct B end

julia> itr = (x < 5 ? A() : B() for x in 1:10);

julia> eltype(itr)
Any

julia> infer_eltype(itr)
Union{A, B}
```

number.","creator":{"login":"StefanKarpinski","id":153596,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MzU5Ng==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/153596?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski","html_url":"https://github.com/StefanKarpinski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":101,"closed_issues":33,"state":"open","created_at":"2016-08-18T13:36:22Z","updated_at":"2024-06-21T18:20:57Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":14,"created_at":"2014-12-30T07:55:03Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T00:02:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"So this is an issue that I find after figuring out how the keyword arguments are currently implemented in Julia. It will probably not be an issue anymore if #2773 is implemented.

The document on methods says

> Methods are dispatched based only on positional arguments, with keyword arguments processed after the matching method is identified.

However, method dispatch actually behaves differently when doing a function call with or without keyword arguments. i.e.

``` julia
julia> function f(::Integer)
       2
       end
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> function f(::Number; kw...)
       1
       end
f (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> f(1)
2

julia> f(1; a = 2)
1
```

What happens here is that `f.env.kwsorter` only has one method defined and therefore when calling with keyword argument, `f(::Integer)` does not participate in method dispatch.

IMHO, there are several possible ways to fix it,
1. Fix the document to include this behavior. This should be the easiest fix but will probably make the whole keyword argument/optional argument/multiple dispatch more confusing especially for someone who does not know how it all works behind the scene. (It's already quite confusing/surprising that anonymous function does not support keyword argument for someone (like me) that expects python-like keyword argument implementation.)
2. Having an entry (that just throw an error) in `env.kwsorter` even for methods that does not take keyword arguments. This can also avoid the following confusing abuse of overriding method
   
   ``` julia
   julia> function f(::Number; kw...)
          1
          end
   f (generic function with 1 method)
   
   julia> function f(::Number)
          2
          end
   f (generic function with 1 method)
   
   julia> f(1)
   2
   
   julia> f(1; a = 2)
   1
   ```
   
   This is probably the easiest way to fix the code and is consistent with the best long term behavior.
3. Fix #2773 and let the method themselves handle keyword argument dispatch. Since #2773 is on `1.0` milestone, hopefully this will be eventually be implemented.

{"action":"opened","number":54909,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54909","id":1934365006,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5zTBFO","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54909","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54909.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54909.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54909","number":54909,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Create `infer_eltype` function","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Addresses #54157 

This PR adds a function which can be useful when one wants to infer the eltype of a collection with only a loose `eltype` but which could be made tighter by using type inference, for example:

```julia
julia> struct A end

julia> struct B end

julia> itr = (x < 5 ? A() : B() for x in 1:10);

julia> eltype(itr)
Any

julia> infer_eltype(itr)
Union{A, B}
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","id":2254049505,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6GWhDh","number":54157,"title":"Create a function to use type inference for eltype","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":29,"created_at":"2024-04-19T21:45:29Z","updated_at":"2024-06-23T23:06:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm using this function to compute the type generically for iterators:

```julia
function compute_eltype(iter)
    T = eltype(iter)
    return T === Any ? Base.@default_eltype(iter) : T
end
```

and I'm asking myself if this couldn't be applied by default to some generators and iterators which currently returns `Any` (actually if it is applied specifically to just those types it can be used only `Base.@default_eltype(iter)` I think)

For example consider

```julia
julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))
Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Tuple{Int64}, Vector{Int64}}}(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64

julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#3#4"}(var"#3#4"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64
```

Performance-wise I see a cost of a couple of ns. I found that there is this other issue and associated PR about a special case of this: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48249 and https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48277, but I think that a function as above could be applied more generally. Would this be okay? Or is this problematic?

#54157 

This PR adds a function which can be useful when one wants to infer the eltype of a collection with only a loose `eltype` but which could be made tighter by using type inference, for example:

```julia
julia> struct A end

julia> struct B end

julia> itr = (x < 5 ? A() : B() for x in 1:10);

julia> eltype(itr)
Any

julia> infer_eltype(itr)
Union{A, B}
```

#54157 

This PR adds a function which can be useful when one wants to infer the eltype of a collection with only a loose `eltype` but which could be made tighter by using type inference, for example:

```julia
julia> struct A end

julia> struct B end

julia> itr = (x < 5 ? A() : B() for x in 1:10);

julia> eltype(itr)
Any

julia> infer_eltype(itr)
Union{A, B}
```

```julia

struct A{R}
    state::Float64
    v::R
    A(state, v::R) where {R} = new{R}(state, v)
    A{R}(state) where {R} = new{R}(state) # only difference
end

struct B{R}
    state::Float64
    v::R
    B(state, v::R) where {R} = new{R}(state, v)
end

function update_all!(s)
    X = typeof(s).name.wrapper
    for x in 1:10^7
        s = X(s.state + x, s.v)
    end
    return s
end

a = A(0.0, Int[1])
b = B(0.0, Int[1])

update_all!(a);
update_all!(b);
@time update_all!(a);
@time update_all!(b);
```

which gives

```julia
julia> @time update_all!(a);
  0.050859 seconds (10.00 M allocations: 305.176 MiB, 6.75% gc time)

julia> @time update_all!(b);
  0.007372 seconds (1 allocation: 32 bytes)
```

The only difference between `A` and `B` is that I have a parametric inner constructor in `A`, and somehow this affects the performance of constructing instances. I initially thought it was an `Accessors` issue (https://github.com/JuliaObjects/Accessors.jl/issues/148) but then @aplavin pointed out it was actually something happening even without it.

to make an unhelpful error message more helpful","user":{"login":"DavidSagan","id":1026006,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwMjYwMDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1026006?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan","html_url":"https://github.com/DavidSagan","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DavidSagan/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":163368715,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjMzNjg3MTU=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/parser","name":"parser","color":"bfdadc","default":false,"description":"Language parsing and surface syntax"},{"id":1611077843,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjExMDc3ODQz","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/error%20messages","name":"error messages","color":"f0f725","default":false,"description":"Better, more actionable error messages"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-12-30T07:08:20Z","updated_at":"2024-05-01T21:04:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Using: Julia 1.10.0
Consider the following test file:
```
function f1()
  if true
  endif
end

function f2()
end
```

There is a typo in `f1` and `endif` should be `end`. Loading this file generates the error message:
```
julia> include("z.jl")
ERROR: LoadError: ParseError:
# Error @ /Users/dcs16/z.jl:7:5
function f2()
end
#  
└ ── Expected `end`
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ ~/z.jl:7
 [2] include(fname::String)
   @ Base.MainInclude ./client.jl:489
 [3] top-level scope
   @ REPL[5]:1
in expression starting at /Users/dcs16/z.jl:7
```
The problem is that the line number given by the error message is the line at the end of the file. In a real life situation the file could be hundreds or more lines long so the error message is fairly useless in terms of localizing the error. Trying to find where the error can thus be a lengthy process of dividing the file into half, testing, and then further dividing, etc., until the error is localized.

As a feature request, if it were possible to modify the error message so that it gave where the unmatched block started (line 2 in this example) this would be very useful and substantially cut down on debugging {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54326","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54326/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54326/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54326/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54326","id":2274339959,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6Hj6x3","number":54326,"title":"Improve error message when unpacking in loop is built without paranthesis","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-05-01T23:23:24Z","updated_at":"2024-05-01T23:23:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Now:

```julia
julia> [x for x, y in [[1,2], [2,3]]]
ERROR: ParseError:
# Error @ REPL[10]:1:9
[x for x, y in [[1,2], [2,3]]]
#       └ ── invalid iteration spec: expected one of `=` `in` or `∈`
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

the suggestion seems off because the `in` is already there, it should somehow hint you to the right direction e.g. putting `(x, y)`.

Actually, but maybe this is controversial, a syntax like this seems good to me, and maybe it could be allowed, in Python it works both with and without parenthesis and also in Julia `x, y = [2, 3]` already works so it seems good for consistency.

Julia 1.10-rc1:

```julia
julia> f(x, y, z) = x+y+z
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> f(3,4 5)
ERROR: ParseError:
# Error @ REPL[2]:1:7
f(3,4 5)
#     ╙ ── Expected `)`
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

Contrast this with Python 3.10 which instead:

```python
>>> def f(x,y,z): return x+y+z
... 
>>> f(3,4 5)
  File "<stdin>", line 1
    f(3,4 5)
        ^^^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax. Perhaps you forgot a comma?
```

The error message of Python seems better, can we change the Julia message to something similar? Probably multiple dispatch makes this trickier though.

Indeed it was better in Julia 1.9

```julia
julia> f(3,4 5)
ERROR: syntax: missing comma or ) in argument list
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

It would be easy in this case

```julia
function iterate(r::OrdinalRange{T}, i) where {T}
    @inline
    i >= last(r) && return nothing # >= instead of ==
    next = convert(T, i + step(r))
    (next, next)
end
```

```julia
function iterate(r::OrdinalRange{T}, i) where {T}
    @inline
    rf, rl = first(r), last(r)
    rf < rl && (i < rf || i >= rl) && return nothing
    rf >= rl && (i <= rl || i > rf) && return nothing
    next = convert(T, i + step(r))
    (next, next)
end
```

I would have assumed that this is more costly but I see on 1.10 and 1.11 this:

```julia
julia> function f(x)
           s = 0
           for i in 1:x
               s += rand() < 0.5 ? 1 : 2
           end
       end
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @btime f(1000) # before
  581.742 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> @btime f(1000) # after
  489.426 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

which indicates the opposite, does someone know why?

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454","id":2293162453,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6IruHV","number":54454,"title":"Interest for an `Iterators.nth(x, n)` API?","user":{"login":"ghyatzo","id":8601724,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2MDE3MjQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8601724?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo","html_url":"https://github.com/ghyatzo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ghyatzo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-05-13T15:35:45Z","updated_at":"2024-05-14T02:47:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello,

After searching far and wide both in issues, PR and on the discourse, I could not find any discussion about adding an `Iterators.nth(x, n)` API just for ease of use and simplicity. [This](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/package-to-read-process-lines-without-new-allocations/92448/9?u=cshen) is the only other reference about this possibility I could find.

I have played a little bit with it in the past during various projects and ended up with a slight evolution over the basic version mentioned by @stevengj in the linked post, which I am carrying around when needed:
```julia
_inbounds_nth(itr, n) = getindex(iterate(Base.Iterators.drop(itr, n-1)), 1)
_safe_nth(itr, n) = begin
	y = iterate(Base.Iterators.drop(itr, n-1))
	isnothing(y) ? nothing : getindex(y, 1)
end
nth(itr, n; skip_checks=false) = skip_checks ? _inbounds_nth(itr, n) : _safe_nth(itr, n)

simple_nth(itr, n) = first(Iterators.drop(itr, n-1))
```
which offers the ability to skip bounds checking at the expense of a crash (opposed to just returning nothing).
```julia
julia> itr = collect(1:10000)
julia> _safe_nth(itr, 10001)

julia> _inbounds_nth(itr, 10001)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching getindex(::Nothing, ::Int64)
Stacktrace:
 [1] _inbounds_nth(itr::Vector{Int64}, n::Int64)
   @ Main .\REPL[198]:1
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[205]:1
```
but that offers decent performance benefits, although we can't escape the `O(n)` complexity without extra assumptions (not that I know of at least)
```julia
julia> @btime _inbounds_nth(itr, 9999) setup=(itr=collect(1:10000))
  151.222 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
9999

julia> @btime _safe_nth(itr, 9999) setup=(itr=collect(1:10000))
  4.400 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
9999

julia> @btime simple_nth(itr, 9999) setup=(itr=collect(1:10000))
  4.414 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
9999
```
(btw `simple_nth` also errors out when called out of bounds).

Instead of straight up opening a PR I wanted to check if there was any desire for this kind of little QOL pieces of code.
And more importantly, check with much more knowledgeable people a couple of doubts:
- is it better to just throw an error or return nothing for these kinds of APIs?
- maybe instead of a keyword argument `skip_checks` it is possible to "retrofit" the `@inbounds` macro to have something like
`@inbounds Iterators.nth(itr, n)` kind of calls, is that even a good idea?
- On the topic of the question above, is there a better way to avoid the branch in the call to begin with? is it automatically optimized away?
- Should such API just return the whole iteration tuple with the state, and let the user deal with that (doesn't feel right to me though)?
- I don't know enough about all possible edge cases of what "an iterable" is like, therefore my naive `inbounds` version only offers performance benefits in this particular case with a vector, so: is making such distinction even worth at all?
- Maybe useful for #10092?
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2109175028","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454#issuecomment-2109175028","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54454","id":2109175028,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM59t3T0","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-14T02:47:56Z","updated_at":"2024-05-14T02:47:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I just note that

```julia
_safe_nth(itr, n) = begin
    y = iterate(Base.Iterators.drop(itr, n-1))
    ifelse(isnothing(y), nothing, getindex(y, 1))
end
```

is as fast as your `_inbounds_nth`. 

```julia
julia> @btime _safe_nth(itr, 9999) setup=(itr=collect(1:10000))
  161.977 ns (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
9999
```

Actually I'm a bit confused why the normal branching has a so high cost.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2109175028/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```julia
julia> itr = Iterators.filter(x -> x != 10, 1:10000);

julia> @btime _inbounds_nth($itr, 9999);
  7.086 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> @btime _safe_nth($itr, 9999);
  7.083 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

In any case I think that returning only the element and not a new iterator starting from there is not ideal because usually one wants to go on with the iteration afterwards so I would consider something like:

```julia
nth(itr, n) = Iterators.peel(Iterators.drop(itr, n-1))

julia> @btime nth($itr, 9999);
  7.086 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

```julia
mutable struct B
    x::Union{Float64, Tuple{Float64, Float64}}
    y::Union{Float64, Tuple{Float64, Float64}}
end

ex = B[B(rand(), (rand(),rand())) for _ in 1:10^5];
```

now if you execute:

```julia
julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7776776

julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7820648

julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7923000

julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7911824
```

This can become very extreme in some situation, I saw a 2x difference between runs on a more complex vector of structs.

Tested in 1.10 and nightly, same behaviour

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958","id":2226446500,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6EtOCk","number":53958,"title":"Performance of function totally changes if it first errors?","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-04-04T20:27:07Z","updated_at":"2024-04-04T20:27:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I was reading the thread in https://discourse.julialang.org/t/julia-slower-than-python-for-finding-pangrams/112481 and I found a very strange behaviour, consider:

```julia
input = ""

function ispangram10(input::String)
                  a,A,z,Z = UInt8('a'),UInt8('A'),UInt8('z'),UInt8('Z')
                  appears::UInt32 = ~((one(UInt32)<<26)-one(UInt32))
                  @inbounds for b in codeunits(input)
                      if A ≤ b ≤ Z
                          appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-A)
                      elseif a ≤ b ≤ z
                          appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-a)
                      end
                      appears == (-1%UInt32) && return true
                  end
                  false
              end
           
ispangram10(input) # this will error

shift_safe(::Type{T}, s::Integer) where {T} = s & (8 * sizeof(T) - 1)

ispangram10(input)
@time ispangram10(input)
```

you will see that the last timing is

```julia
julia> @time ispangram10(input)
  0.000032 seconds (137 allocations: 2.141 KiB)
```

now if you instead run this:

```julia
input = ""

function ispangram10(input::String)
                  a,A,z,Z = UInt8('a'),UInt8('A'),UInt8('z'),UInt8('Z')
                  appears::UInt32 = ~((one(UInt32)<<26)-one(UInt32))
                  @inbounds for b in codeunits(input)
                      if A ≤ b ≤ Z
                          appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-A)
                      elseif a ≤ b ≤ z
                          appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-a)
                      end
                      appears == (-1%UInt32) && return true
                  end
                  false
              end
           
#ispangram10(input) # this is the only difference, it is commented in this version

shift_safe(::Type{T}, s::Integer) where {T} = s & (8 * sizeof(T) - 1)

ispangram10(input)
@time ispangram10(input)
```

it returns instead

```julia
julia> @time ispangram10(input)
  0.000002 seconds
```

What is it happening here? Why the error in the first version makes the function perf much worse afterewards?

```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.2
Commit bd47eca2c8a (2024-03-01 10:14 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 12 × AMD Ryzen 5 5600H with Radeon Graphics
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, znver3)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 12 virtual cores)
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","id":2254049505,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6GWhDh","number":54157,"title":"Use `Base.@default_eltype` for  iterators and generators `eltype` function which currently returns `Any`?","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-04-19T21:45:29Z","updated_at":"2024-04-19T21:45:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm using this function to compute the type generically for iterators where currently `eltype` returns `Any`:

```julia
function compute_eltype(iter)
    T = eltype(iter)
    return T === Any ? Base.@default_eltype(iter) : T
end
```

and I'm asking myself if this couldn't be applied by default to some generators and iterators which currently returns `Any` (actually if it is applied specifically to just some types it can be used only `Base.@default_eltype(iter)`

For example consider

```julia
julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))
Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Tuple{Int64}, Vector{Int64}}}(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64

julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#3#4"}(var"#3#4"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64
```

I found that there is this other issue and associated PR about a special case of this: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48249 and https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48277, but I think that a function as above could be applied more generally. Would this be okay? Or is this problematic?

julia> mutable struct ConstIdentity
       end

julia> dict = Dict{ConstIdentity, Int64}()
Dict{ConstIdentity, Int64}()

julia> c1 = ConstIdentity()
ConstIdentity()

julia> c2 = ConstIdentity()
ConstIdentity()

julia> dict[c1] = 1
1

julia> dict[c2] = 2
2

julia> haskey(dict, c1)
true

julia> dict2 = deepcopy(dict)
Dict{ConstIdentity, Int64} with 2 entries:
  ConstIdentity() => 1
  ConstIdentity() => 2

julia> haskey(dict2, c1)
false
```

The identity-only-mutables in the keys got copied, so their hash values changed and the resulting dict is broken. I think it would be better if `deepcopy` preserved the data structure {"action":"opened","number":54208,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54208","id":1835643997,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5tabRd","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54208","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54208.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54208.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54208","number":54208,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Refine eltype of `Base.Generator` when its type is concrete","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Tries to address #54157 

Now:

```julia
julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#1#2"}(var"#1#2"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Int64
```

Before

```julia
julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#1#2"}(var"#1#2"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any
```

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-04-23T00:09:41Z","updated_at":"2024-04-23T00:58:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Many packages, such as Dictionaries.jl, StaticArrays.jl or FixedSizeArrays.jl, need access to Julia's type inference functionality to be able to offer their users convenient construction of their custom data structures from generators. This is how comprehensions that create `Array`s work, after all, so it only seems fair to enable user packages to use type inference, too.

Exposing type inference to users is, however, dangerous; because the type inference works on a *best-effort* basis, it's not reliably exact, so users must not rely on the exactness of the type inference for correctness of their code and APIs. A peculiar fact is that type inference may give different results for the same queries at different points during the run of Julia. These issues are subtle, and users are overconfident.

Functionality like `Core.Compiler.return_type`, `Base.return_types` or `Base.@default_eltype` is widely used in the ecosystem, even though these are Julia-internal implementation details not subject to any compatibility guarantee.

Could we have a user-facing type inference API, provided it came with enough documentation and warnings to enable it to be used correctly?

Realistically, the type inference functionality is already widely (mis)used, so IMO it's clear that such documentation is necessary. And, once the docs exist, I guess that providing an interface that's actually permitted for use would be a no-brainer?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54207/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54207/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2071206096","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54207#issuecomment-2071206096","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54207","id":2071206096,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM57dBjQ","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-23T00:58:44Z","updated_at":"2024-04-23T00:58:44Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Probably Now:

```julia
julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#1#2"}(var"#1#2"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Int64
```

Before

```julia
julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#1#2"}(var"#1#2"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","id":2254049505,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6GWhDh","number":54157,"title":"Use `Base.@default_eltype` for  iterators and generators `eltype` function which currently returns `Any`?","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-04-19T21:45:29Z","updated_at":"2024-04-23T12:08:13Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm using this function to compute the type generically for iterators:

```julia
function compute_eltype(iter)
    T = eltype(iter)
    return T === Any ? Base.@default_eltype(iter) : T
end
```

and I'm asking myself if this couldn't be applied by default to some generators and iterators which currently returns `Any` (actually if it is applied specifically to just those types it can be used only `Base.@default_eltype(iter)` I think)

For example consider

```julia
julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))
Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Tuple{Int64}, Vector{Int64}}}(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64

julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#3#4"}(var"#3#4"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64
```

Performance-wise I see a cost of a couple of ns. I found that there is this other issue and associated PR about a special case of this: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48249 and https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48277, but I think that a function as above could be applied more generally. Would this be okay? Or is this problematic?

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","id":2254049505,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6GWhDh","number":54157,"title":"Use `Base.@default_eltype` for  iterators and generators `eltype` function which currently returns `Any`?","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-04-19T21:45:29Z","updated_at":"2024-04-23T18:00:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm using this function to compute the type generically for iterators:

```julia
function compute_eltype(iter)
    T = eltype(iter)
    return T === Any ? Base.@default_eltype(iter) : T
end
```

and I'm asking myself if this couldn't be applied by default to some generators and iterators which currently returns `Any` (actually if it is applied specifically to just those types it can be used only `Base.@default_eltype(iter)` I think)

For example consider

```julia
julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))
Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Tuple{Int64}, Vector{Int64}}}(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64

julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#3#4"}(var"#3#4"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64
```

Performance-wise I see a cost of a couple of ns. I found that there is this other issue and associated PR about a special case of this: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48249 and https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48277, but I think that a function as above could be applied more generally. Would this be okay? Or is this problematic?


","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2073051711","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157#issuecomment-2073051711","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54157","id":2073051711,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM57kEI_","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-23T18:00:01Z","updated_at":"2024-04-23T18:00:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"A thing I don't understand is why in `Base.@default_eltype` (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/array.jl#L744) it is used `promote_typejoin_union(T)`, I would prefer to infer a `Union` of concrete types than an abstract type e.g.

```julia
julia> s = (x < 5 ? x : 2.0*x for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#5#6"}(var"#5#6"(), 1:10)

julia> T = Core.Compiler.return_type(Base._iterator_upper_bound, Tuple{typeof(s)})
Union{Float64, Int64}

julia> Base.promote_typejoin_union(T)
Real
```

Why can't we use `Core.Compiler.return_type(Base._iterator_upper_bound, Tuple{typeof(s)})` at least when the `Union` is not too big? Notice that this affects also `collect`:

```julia
julia> collect(s)
10-element Vector{Real}:
...
```

```julia
function compute_eltype(iter)
    T = eltype(iter)
    return T === Any ? Base.@default_eltype(iter) : T
end
```

and I'm asking myself if this couldn't be applied by default to some generators and iterators which currently returns `Any` (actually if it is applied specifically to just those types it can be used only `Base.@default_eltype(iter)` I think)

For example consider

```julia
julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))
Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Tuple{Int64}, Vector{Int64}}}(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64

julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#3#4"}(var"#3#4"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64
```

Performance-wise I see a cost of a couple of ns. I found that there is this other issue and associated PR about a special case of this: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48249 and https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48277, but I think that a function as above could be applied more generally. Would this be okay? Or is this problematic?

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54272","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54272/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54272/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54272/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54272","id":2266071921,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6HEYNx","number":54272,"title":"Creating struct instances is slow when another inner constructor returns `undef` fields","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-04-26T15:54:46Z","updated_at":"2024-04-27T12:55:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:

```julia

struct A{R}
    state::Float64
    v::R
    A(state, v::R) where {R} = new{R}(state, v)
    A{R}(state) where {R} = new{R}(state) # only difference
end

struct B{R}
    state::Float64
    v::R
    B(state, v::R) where {R} = new{R}(state, v)
end

function update_all!(s)
    X = typeof(s).name.wrapper
    for x in 1:10^7
        s = X(s.state + x, s.v)
    end
    return s
end

a = A(0.0, Int[1])
b = B(0.0, Int[1])

update_all!(a);
update_all!(b);
@time update_all!(a);
@time update_all!(b);
```

which gives

```julia
julia> @time update_all!(a);
  0.050859 seconds (10.00 M allocations: 305.176 MiB, 6.75% gc time)

julia> @time update_all!(b);
  0.007372 seconds (1 allocation: 32 bytes)
```

The only difference between `A` and `B` is that I have a parametric inner constructor in `A`, and somehow this affects the performance of constructing instances. I initially thought it was an `Accessors` issue (https://github.com/JuliaObjects/Accessors.jl/issues/148) but then @aplavin pointed out it was actually something happening even without it.

```julia
function compute_eltype(iter)
    T = eltype(iter)
    return T === Any ? Base.@default_eltype(iter) : T
end
```

and I'm asking myself if this couldn't be applied by default to some generators and iterators which currently returns `Any` (actually if it is applied specifically to just those types it can be used only `Base.@default_eltype(iter)` I think)

For example consider

```julia
julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))
Base.Iterators.Flatten{Tuple{Tuple{Int64}, Vector{Int64}}}(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> iter = Iterators.flatten(((3,), [4, 5]))

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64

julia> iter = (x^2 for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#3#4"}(var"#3#4"(), 1:10)

julia> eltype(iter)
Any

julia> compute_eltype(iter)
Int64
```

Performance-wise I see a cost of a couple of ns. I found that there is this other issue and associated PR about a special case of this: https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48249 and https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/48277, but I think that a function as above could be applied more generally. Would this be okay? Or is this problematic?

```julia
julia> struct A end

julia> struct B end

julia> itr = (x < 5 ? A() : B() for x in 1:10)
Base.Generator{UnitRange{Int64}, var"#1#2"}(var"#1#2"(), 1:10)

julia> Base.@default_eltype(itr)
Any

julia> Core.Compiler.return_type(Base._iterator_upper_bound, Tuple{typeof(itr)})
Union{A, B}
```

+## ["Unionize" collections](@id unionize-collections)
+
+When working e.g with agent based models or finite elements with varying element geometries, a common pattern is the occurence of collections (e.g. Vectors) of objects of different types on which one wants to perform certain actions depending on their type. By default, the element type of a vector of objects of different struct types is a common supertype, often `Any`. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53224","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53224/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53224/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53224/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53224","id":2121952337,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5-emxR","number":53224,"title":"Extending Base.convert is ambiguous on 1.8/1.9 but not on 1.10 and master","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-07T00:57:07Z","updated_at":"2024-02-07T00:57:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I was doing a PR in one package when this error appear in the tests:

```julia
   Got exception outside of a @test
  MethodError: convert(::Type{Union{MixedStructTypes.Uninitialized, Tuple{Float64, Float64}}}, ::Tuple{Float64, Float64}) is ambiguous. Candidates:
    convert(::Type{T}, x::T) where T in Base at Base.jl:61
    convert(::Type{Union{MixedStructTypes.Uninitialized, T}}, x) where T in MixedStructTypes at /home/runner/work/
```

the fact is that this is instead non-ambiguous in 1.10 and master: see https://github.com/JuliaDynamics/MixedStructTypes.jl/actions/runs/7808260779/job/21298161455. So what is the correct behaviour?

This is what I did to extend the convert function:

```julia
Base.convert(::Type{Union{MixedStructTypes.Uninitialized, T}}, x) where T = convert(T, x)
```

I didn't find an MWE unfortunately

Julia 1.9.3:
```julia
julia> a = Union{Int,String}[1]; a[1] = 2.0
ERROR: MethodError: Cannot `convert` an object of type
  Float64 to an object of type
  Union{Int, String}
```

This seems odd to me, because `a = [1]; a[1] = 2.0` works, as does `a = Integer[1]; a[1] = 2.0`. In particular, in this last example, I read this as "`2.0 isa Integer` is `false`, so convert `2.0` into an object `x` for which `x isa Integer` is `true`" (or something like that), and it seems like similar logic should apply when replacing `Integer` with `Union{Int,String}` (or other unions).

In my particular case, I am adding an `InlineStrings.String15` into an array of type `Vector{Union{Int,String}}`, and it would be nice if Julia did the conversion to `String` for me ```

when I use `@time` on some other macro it doesn't seem to work correctly:

```julia
julia> using MixedStructTypes

julia> @time @compact_struct_type A begin

                 struct C
                     y
                 end
             end
  0.001524 seconds (313 allocations: 23.852 KiB)
```

It actually takes something like 1 second to finish on my machine.

Tested on both 1.10 and nightly. I'm not sure on a simpler reproducer, but it happens also with other macros, e.g. `@sum_type` from SumTypes.jl

Julia"},{"id":6410585030,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABkWpM8AAAABfhnDxg","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/observability","name":"observability","color":"D7AAF2","default":false,"description":"metrics, timing, understandability, reflection, logging, ..."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-01-26T01:21:12Z","updated_at":"2024-02-12T01:15:16Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:


```julia
mutable struct B
    x::Union{Float64, Tuple{Float64, Float64}}
    y::Union{Float64, Tuple{Float64, Float64}}
end

ex = B[B(rand(), (rand(),rand())) for _ in 1:10^5];
```

now if you execute:

```julia
julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7776776

julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7820648

julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7923000

julia> Base.summarysize(ex)
7911824
```

This can become very extreme in some situation, I saw a 2x difference between runs on a more complex vector of structs.

Tested in 1.10 and nightly, same behaviour
